docs.title: All-in-One Small Scale RWD Drift Guide
docs.text:
This is the beginning of a long-term project.

Small-scale RWD drifting has grown organically — through experiments, Facebook posts, private messages, trial and error, and a lot of undocumented knowledge. Finding reliable information often means digging through years of posts and opinions.

This guide exists to slowly bring that information into one place.

docs.title: What is this right now?
docs.text:
At this stage, this site is not finished and not complete.

The main completed section at the moment is:

docs.title: 📜 History
docs.text:
A researched timeline of how RWD entered small-scale drifting — from early experiments to the pre-COVID era.

This was the starting point of the project and the foundation for everything else.

docs.title: What is planned next
docs.text: The project is being built step by step. Planned sections include:

docs.title: 🧩 Chassis Catalogue
docs.text:
A structured catalogue of small-scale RWD chassis: - CNC / production kits - 3D-printed and independent designs - limited runs and experimental platforms

This is currently work in progress and expanding gradually.

docs.title: 🧠 Terminology
docs.text:
Clear explanations of commonly misunderstood concepts: - camber, toe, caster - KPI, scrub radius - pro/anti torque, pro/anti squat - suspension and steering geometry

Some pages already exist, many more are planned.

docs.title: 🚀 Beginner Guides
docs.text: Practical information for newcomers: - what electronics are actually required - compatibility pitfalls - realistic expectations by budget - how to choose a chassis without relying on opinions alone

docs.title: Why this project exists
docs.text:
Because the same questions are asked again and again.

Because valuable knowledge disappears in chat history.

Because newcomers deserve something better than contradictory answers.

And because small-scale RWD drifting has a history and depth worth documenting.

docs.title: Work in progress
docs.text:
Everything here is evolving.

Information will be added, corrected, refined, and expanded over time. Some data may be incomplete or missing — especially for rare or undocumented chassis.

This is expected.

docs.title: Want to help?
docs.text:
If you have: - corrections - photos - missing information - archived posts or documentation

You’re welcome to contribute.

See Contribute for details.

Thanks for reading — and welcome at the very beginning.

docs.title: Choosing Your First Chassis
docs.text:
1. If you have no previous experience with RC drift or other RC categories, I would not recommend starting with the most expensive chassis available.

Even if a high-end kit keeps its resale value, you may still damage it simply because you lack experience. There is also a chance that the hobby may not be for you, so going all-in from day one is not always the smartest choice.

If you end up loving the hobby, you will almost certainly want another chassis sooner or later anyway. Starting with a more beginner-friendly option is not a waste — it gives you the experience to properly build, tune and drive a better kit later.

2. If you have absolutely no RC experience and do not feel confident with mechanics, your safest choice is usually an RTR or ARTR car with a working factory setup. A used car can also be a good option, but without experience it may be difficult to tell whether the car has a problem, or whether you simply do not yet have the skill to drive it properly.

3. Many people ask: “What is the best chassis?” There is no single correct answer.

A perfectly machined, high-end CNC kit with top electronics can still perform badly if it is assembled or tuned incorrectly. At the same time, an experienced driver with a DIY K969 conversion built from spare parts may drive with better angle, consistency and control.

Chassis can be compared by precision, fitment quality, adjustability, parts support, availability and value. But some kits require more understanding and are better suited to experienced builders.

Try not to overwhelm yourself with too many advanced tuning options at the beginning. More adjustment is not always better if you do not yet understand what those adjustments do. Beginners often assume that buying upgrades will automatically improve the car. In reality, many upgrades are cosmetic, while others simply unlock additional tuning options. Without understanding how to use those options, an upgrade can sometimes make the car perform worse. In fact, a simpler chassis with fewer tuning options is often easier to learn and can provide a much more enjoyable experience for beginners.

Build your experience one step at a time. Learn to drive first, then learn to tune, then decide what kind of chassis philosophy fits you best.

Take a look at the Catalogue (WIP). It is not intended to tell you what to buy. Instead, it is designed to help you compare different chassis, understand their strengths, weaknesses and available adjustments, and make an informed decision based on your own needs and budget.

It can also be used simply as a historical reference to see how small scale RWD drift chassis evolved over time.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
AE28U is Ark Edge's first product and it faced mixed feelings at first. The price point was very high, and since it was an unproven brand, hobbyists were cautious. It looked different from most chassis on the market and initially felt somewhat out of sync with the direction of the hobby. At a time when many manufacturers and enthusiasts were increasingly focused on 1:24 model kits, Ark Edge released a chassis capable of supporting wheelbases as short as 86 mm, giving considerable attention to MINI-Z sized bodies.

Rather than ignoring the skepticism, the Ark Edge team actively sought contact with the community worldwide, requesting feedback, releasing update parts and sending them free of charge to customers who had already purchased the kit. Still, many enthusiasts, although already believing in the quality of the parts and accepting that the brand is trustworthy, were still uncertain, because there was very little footage showing how the AE28U actually performed on track. Several well-known hobbyists initially questioned the AE28U's drifting performance.

However, Ark Edge stood their ground once more, shared through social media and were active enough, persistent enough to slowly earn their reputation. They also released their own gyro, which went a bit viral for a while, helping increase the brand's visibility within the community.

While the AE28U never became the dominant chassis many expected, it established Ark Edge as a serious manufacturer. The lessons learned from the AE28U directly influenced the development of the AE24X, a chassis that would go on to receive significantly broader recognition within the community.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
After releasing several seperate upgrades, the first evolution of DRZ2 was released in june, 2021.

DRZ2 Limited Edition, or simply DRZ2LE featuring:

Adjustable wheelbase from 94 to 114 milimeters and longer front arms and extendable rear arms to fit 1/24 bodies.

New front bulkhead, brass side body mount, brass battery mount and optional aluminum servo mount.

Later, optional aluminum gearbox, rear knuckles, and rear arms were also introduced.

DRZ2.1LE was the next evolution of DRZ2 platform offering:

New front knuckles, aluminum steering crank with more adjustability, dog-leg steering linkages, rear shocks and axles.

These changes improved steering lock and allowed a softer rear end suspension resulting in more predictable handling.

One of the first independent designs to appear, BRP became famous at local grounds.

The first prototype version was presented in October 2018.

Two months later, in december 2018, the V2 was released.

The third version was released in august 2019, and it became very popular.

December 2020, Team 2k release carbon fiber conversion kit, compatible with BRPV2 and BRPV3, including lower deck, upper deck and battery box.

April 2021, Team 2k presented BRP IV made of carbon fiber and aluminum.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Interesting facts:

  • the kit comes with tools for assembly, including nails, tweezers, screwdriver etc.

  • a few different spring sets are included

  • DriftART2 had many versions, until it evolved to DriftART3

The DriftArt2 chassis...no it feels wrong calling it just a chassis, because it's not! It started as one, but it evolved in an ecosystem with improvements, op parts, experimental solutions and more. You could see two DriftART2 chassis and they could be totally different. And not just visually or updated materials. New systems were introduced, the way certain things work was revised. DriftART2 was born as a chassis, but it became a constantly evolving idea of chasing the ultimate design, hunger for more innovation and aiming for perfection.

Evolution chronology:

  • February 2021: some aluminum parts. Limited DriftART2 Spring Edition with 7075 aluminum gearbox and teasers of other future upgrades.

  • April 2021: pre-assembled version and more teasers.

  • May-July 2021: many updates were introduced and that led to the DriftART2.5 Summer Edition release.

  • October 2021: DriftART2.5 Classic Edition release with simplified 3D steering system comparable to DVF, quick adjustable rods, Kpi steering knuckles, option to install break disks etc.

  • January 2022: new servo seat with angle adjustment, piston shocks.

  • May 2022: B.R.S body roll system, UX-CVD extendable steel CVD DA2.5CC Plus and DA2.5EX Editions.

  • August 2022: RGB roll gearbox from 7075 aluminum

I am not even covering all the upgrades , as wide arms and others and it's still a lot as you can see.

  • January 2023: the successor of DriftART2 was released DriftART3S

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
The consistent releasing of upgrades and innovations became the trademark of DriftART.

The DA3S kit is also offered as factory assembled version.

Some of the upgrades to mention are:

  • Offset Master makes it possible to reduce the effect of wheels offset, achieving less scrub radius. It can reduce offset 2 down to 0

  • Angle Master has multiple ballhead screw slots, allowing much more control over steering geometry, such as KPI, scrub radius and maximum steering angle

  • Moonwalk changes the front suspension from multilink to double wishbone. It allows the caster and camber angles to be adjusted by sliding the upper arms rods instead of using threaded rods, which is way faster

  • **DA4 Conversion kit came out in July 2024

The successor of DA3S is the current DriftART kit: DriftART4

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
G-Force D1 has been promoted with 3 versions to choose from:

  • D1 Lite: All plastic
  • D1-R: Plastic and carbon fiber
  • D1 Pro: Aluminum and carbon fiber, as for the first batch, the customers were given the options to choose the anodizing color of the aluminum parts

I've not found trusted number for amount of D1 kits produced yet, but the kit was known to have some issues, inclucind QC.

The community put the author under pressure for taking too long to solve some cases and the brand quickly lost the trust for a while. But at the end, G-Force kept their word and saved their name. Later, their journey continued with new kit, produced by NEXX Racing, called G-Force TT24

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Onisiki Kodama shares many similarities with the BMR-X PRO.

The gearbox appears to be identical. However, Kodama uses a direct drive steering system with stepless Ackermann adjustment, trading off the stepless caster adjustment found on the BMR-X PRO. Instead, caster is adjusted using shims.

Kodama's battery is positioned behind the gearbox, placing more weight toward the rear and helping generate rear grip.

For narrower track width setups, Kodama can switch from a dual front shock layout to a front monoshock configuration. This clears additional space around the outer front wheel, helping preserve steering angle without binding.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
MA Racing had already established a reputation for exceptional quality with its original chassis, as well as its smaller sibling, the D32. This reputation played a significant role in the community's willingness to embrace the D24's premium price tag.

Having already raised the bar themselves, expectations among small-scale drifters were exceptionally high. D24 not only met those expectations but exceeded them, becoming one of the most praised and desirable chassis of its era — a reputation it continues to hold to this day.

Its stunning aesthetics, later enhanced by the introduction of red and purple editions, made the chassis even more desirable. MA also released a limited run of 30 black-and-green units, which have since become among the rarest D24 Variants. Combined with predictable handling and outstanding on-track performance, D24 delivered an experience that felt complete in every aspect.

MA Racing D24 proved that capturing the hearts of RC enthusiasts does not always require revolutionary new concepts. Sometimes, perfecting existing ideas through exceptional engineering, machining quality, fitment, and execution is enough.

Among its many strengths, the suspension system stood out in particular. The full-metal shock construction with Teflon coating delivered exceptionally smooth operation and was regarded by many as one of the finest at the time.

D24+ was released in January 2025 in red or purple with optional parts including aluminum chassis plate, body mount, copper rear lower arm mount for 0° toe and brakes kit with color matching calipers. The front upper arms design was updated.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Following the success of the OG MA Racing chassis, D32 and D24, expectations for MA Racing's next platform were exceptionally high. Rather than continuing to refine the formula established by its predecessors, the company introduced a chassis that felt noticeably different from anything it had released before.

The DLR8 quickly drew comparisons to Yokomo MD1 platform in 1:10 scale. From its overall layout and high-CG gearbox design to its visual appearance, many hobbyists saw it as the closest thing to a "micro MD1" and eagerly anticipated its release.

Its most distinctive feature was the suspension architecture. Unlike conventional designs, all suspension arms were mounted through ball joints connected by turnbuckles. This allowed precise adjustment of arm movement while minimizing friction and binding. The concept was unusual for the scale and became one of the chassis' defining characteristics.

The drivetrain also departed from previous MA Racing designs. The mid-high motor position and internal idler gear configuration focused on pro-torque behavior, while an optional external gear allowed conversion to anti-torque operation. This gave drivers additional flexibility in tuning the chassis' handling characteristics.

Like other MA Racing products, manufacturing quality, fitment and overall execution were widely praised. However, the internal idler gear bearings proved to be a weak point on some early kits. Owners experiencing issues typically resolved them by replacing them with two 681X bearings.

The DLR8 demonstrated MA Racing's willingness to experiment with new concepts rather than simply refining existing designs. Its distinctive layout and suspension architecture made it one of the most recognizable chassis released by the company.

Some of the optional upgrade parts available for DLR8 included:

  • Blue stainless steel upper deck

  • Extendable dogbones

  • Belt drive conversion

  • Purple brake kit with matching calipers

  • Copper rear arm mounts

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
The original MA Racing platform was the company's first chassis and marked the beginning of what would become one of the most respected names in small-scale drifting. Rather than waiting until a fully CNC-machined design was ready, MA Racing introduced a mechanically capable platform built primarily from 3D-printed nylon and carbon fiber.

Instead of replacing the chassis with an entirely new design, the platform evolved through successive revisions that gradually introduced CNC aluminum components. Existing owners could upgrade their chassis over time, allowing both the platform and the brand to mature together.

By late 2022, the platform had completed its transition into a premium CNC chassis widely recognized for its exceptional manufacturing quality. The reputation established by the original MA Racing platform laid the foundation for every model that followed, including the D32, D24 and DLR8..

docs.title: Evolution
docs.text:
MA Racing V1 released in December 2021.

The first production version that established the foundation of the MA Racing platform.

Among the earliest teasers were bearing-mounted front suspension arms, an unusual design for the scale at the time that hinted at the company's engineering direction.

MA Racing v1.1 upgrade

The first CNC aluminum upgrades appeared, including the branded upper deck and aluminum spur gear.

MA Racing V1.2 (February 2022) & V1.3 (May 2022)

By this stage, the transition from 3D-printed nylon toward CNC aluminum was well underway.

During this period, MA Racing also introduced optional upgrades including:

  • CNC brake caliper and disc
  • Rear motor mount
  • Rear straight bridge suspension

V1.4 was released in July 2022.

V1.4 was generally regarded as the platform's first "full aluminum" configuration. New aluminum battery holder, extendable rear arms and additional CNC components completed the transition from the original mixed-material platform into a premium chassis.

The final evolution of the original platform, refining an already mature design before MA Racing moved on to the next generation. It marked the end of the platform that established the company's identity.

docs.text:
• Developed by: Pur Woko

  • Release: January 2020

  • Origin: Indonesia

  • Status: Discontinued

  • Production: Batch

  • Scale: 1/28(could do 1/24 with the adjustable WB chassis)

  • Body mounting: Kyosho / holes for DIY magnetic posts

  • Materials: FDM 3D Printed

docs.title: At-a-glance
docs.text:
• Wheelbase: ✅(with the adjustable chassis. Slim deck version is fixed 94mm)

  • Camber: Front ✅ / Rear ✅

  • Toe: Front ✅ / Rear ❌(✅ V2SE)

  • Caster: ❌

  • Ackermann quick adjustment: ❌

  • Ride height: Front ✅ / Rear ✅

  • Track width: Front ✅ / Rear ❌

  • Front shocks: preload ❌(✅ preload clips can be applied) / angle ❌

  • Rear shocks: preload ❌(✅ preload clips can be applied) / angle ✅

  • Active systems: ✅(V2SE Active rear toe)

  • Motor position: mid ❌ / high ✅(MR version) / rear ✅(RR version)

  • Servo position: ❌

  • Pinion-Spur distance: ✅

  • Front knuckle KPI hinge point: ✅

  • Front knuckle steering linkage hinge point: ✅

  • Steering rack linkage hinge point: ❌

docs.title: Details
docs.text:
• Wheelbase adjustment method: steps

  • Wheelbase range: 90–110 mm(fixed 94mm for slim deck)

  • Track width range: ??–?? mm

  • Caster adjustment: static

  • Ackermann adjustment: steering linkages length/position

  • Rear toe behavior: static / dynamic V2SE

docs.title: Drivetrain
docs.text:
• Gearbox type: gear-driven

  • Motor orientation: transverse

  • Forces: anti-torque

  • Reversible: ❌

  • Differential: unknown

docs.title: Steering
docs.text:
• Steering method: direct

  • Servo position: upper deck

docs.title: Suspension
docs.text:
• Front: double wishbone, independent, 2 shocks

  • Rear: double wishbone / multi-link(V2SE), independent, 2 shocks

  • Shocks type: friction shocks

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
PWK V2 was an evolution of the original PWK platform with the most noticeable changes being:

  • rear motor layout
  • anti-torque gearbox

That resulted in rear biased weight center and pro-squat torque reaction, creating a much more suitable foundation for the active rear toe system introduced late in the V2SE.

PWK V2SE RR SD & PWK V2SE MR kits

The V2SE, SD and RR/MR gave more options for different styles:

  • active rear toe was impressive back then, especially for small scale 3D printed kit
  • the narrow decks are modern even today. Upgrade that aged like a fine wine
  • rear motor or high motor options
  • the kit came with slim deck chassis and adjustable wheelbase chassis

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
K-Enhance Project was born as Wltoys K9 front end conversion:

Then Al Kautsar named the first evolution: Rehope

Third version of rehope was designed, but never released for public.

The creator wanted to use narrow body Nissan Silvia CSP311 '66, but the chassis was too wide, so he had to make the chassis narrower.

That's why he designed K-EP Slimmer

docs.text:

  • Camber: Front ✅ / Rear ✅

docs.text:

docs.title: Drivetrain
docs.text:
• Gearbox type: gear-driven / belt-driven(v-belt) / mixed

  • Motor orientation: transverse

  • Forces: pro-torque / anti-torque

  • Reversible: ❌

  • Differential: Ball

docs.title: Steering
docs.text:
• Steering method: pivoted

  • Steering system: dual bellcrank

  • Servo position: both upper and lower deck fixed

docs.title: Suspension
docs.text:
• Front: double wishbone, independent, 2 cantilever shocks

  • Rear: multi-link, independent, 2 shocks

  • Shocks type: friction shocks

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Zero RW00 is a beautiful, high-quality platform with a few evolutions and can be adjusted in variety of different ways. Zero RC is known for providing one of the most detailed instruction manuals, including setup and tuning recommendations.

RW00S released in may 2024

RW00SR released in may 2025 in a few different versions, including AWD front axles

The platform supports more than 10 shocks, allowing very fine control over suspension behavior throughout the travel range. Multiple drivtrain layouts and extensive possibilities in a single chassis.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Front end redesign with cantilever suspension, that doesn't even have bulkhead. Just bellcrank and arms!

  • Front suspension update: double wishbone, independent, cantilever(pushrod) 2 shocks

  • carbon fiber version came later, equipped with lower deck, upper deck and motor mount.

ADS Sleipnir III

Later, K-EP and Bulan Racing fused, creating the ADS - Advanced Development System.

Then the most renown version of Sleipnir was born: ADS Sleipnir III

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
When TG Racing was first introduced, many hobbyists were skeptical. An aluminum and carbon fiber chassis at such a low price seemed unrealistic. While the early kits had some QC issues and a relatively basic suspension design, they offered exceptional value and quickly gained popularity among beginners.

TG Racing maintained close contact with the community, gathering feedback and refining subsequent releases. This approach helped the company grow into one of the most recognizable brands in small-scale drifting, with multiple chassis platforms released in the years that followed.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
The META was one of the more feature-rich chassis of its time, offering a wide adjustment range together with a large ecosystem of optional parts and upgrades.

Notable optional upgrades included aluminum suspension components, aluminum transmission gears and helical gear sets designed to reduce drivetrain noise and improve smoothness. parts

One of the more practical design details was the rear shock tower, which incorporated integrated cable-routing cutouts to help manage motor wires and keep the chassis layout tidy.

Red-and-black special edition version limited to 100 units

Another distinctive feature was the proprietary TRC META 5-bolt wheel system, designed to replicate the appearance of real automotive hubs and vented brake discs

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
One of the design focuses was to make the car lightweight.

An interesting feature is the spring-loaded servo mount, which allows precise Ackermann adjustment via a screw.

The optional floating gearbox is another interesting feature, which allows the motor pinion and spur gear mesh to self-adjust.

According to Yuan Hao, Tommy RC noted that plastic shocks are smoother than the metal ones, so if you want to upgrade, think of it as a visual upgrade rather than a performance upgrade.

I will also quote Yuan Hao: "The main gear is made from PEEK—a high-end material that’s lightweight, durable, and self-lubricating. Fun fact: PEEK is way more expensive than aluminum, so skip the black aluminum main gear—it’s not worth the downgrade! The PEEK gear runs so quietly, it’s almost on par with belt-driven chassis."

Meta X was available in Pro and Max versions. A very limited Pink Edition, reportedly produced in fewer than 20 units, was released exclusively during MRDC events in China.

Some customers reported QC-related issues and felt that certain design choices were made with cost reduction in mind. However, most of these concerns did not appear to affect the car's performance.

docs.title: How RWD came to small scale drifting
2014 Early experiments – Afada Rioo
docs.text:
When I first started asking myself how RWD drifting actually entered the small-scale RC world, the most obvious assumption was Kyosho Mini-Z. The platform had existed for years, so it felt like the logical origin point.

The reality turned out to be very different.

Digging deeper — post by post, year by year — eventually led back to 2014, inside the Facebook group Mini R/C Drift Hobby Indonesia. Almost everything related to RWD drifting in small scale at that time pointed to a single name:

Afada Rioo.

Scrolling through those early posts today, you can still feel the anticipation. The community was waiting for his next update, his next experiment, his next breakthrough. Afada set himself a challenge — and fully committed to solving it.

By modern standards, the results may look crude or unconventional. But context matters. There were no precise nano electronics, no off-the-shelf solutions, no reference builds. Everything had to be invented from scratch.

Some of the solutions he came up with feel almost surreal even now.

Afada had to learn everything at once — electronics, mechanics, geometry, trial-and-error experimentation. He modified components, rebuilt systems, and continuously adapted ideas that simply didn’t exist in ready-made form at the time.

Looking back, it’s fair to say that Afada Rioo is the grandfather of small-scale RWD drifting. The connection between his early experiments and what exists today is direct — and impossible to ignore.

docs.title: 2016 Legowo Setiawan and his first K989 conversion
docs.text:
Legowo Setiawan came from the 1/10 drift world, where he had already spent time studying chassis design, steering geometry, suspension behavior, and tuning philosophy.

In 2015 he decided to apply that knowledge — along with some of Afada Rioo’s discoveries — and began his own RWD project.

The WLToys K989 is a budget “semi-hobby-grade” chassis, yet in 2016, Setiawan managed to turn it into a functional RWD drift platform.

A year later, in 2017, he successfully converted the Atomic AMR into an RWD drifter as well — and this time, it didn’t go unnoticed.

Let’s return to the importance of Afada Rioo. Look at these two cars side by side — do you recognize the connection?

Legowo’s work caught the attention of Atomic. Their manager, Mr. Kim, initiated development of a dedicated RWD kit based on Legowo’s research.

Two prototypes were built:

  • one sent to Atomic

  • one kept by Legowo for continued development

According to Legowo, both DRZ1 and DRZ2 were direct results of this collaboration. He later shared that in late 2018 Atomic unilaterally ended the agreement and never responded to his final message.

I also attempted to contact Mr. Kim — without success.

Regardless of how the story ended, one thing remains certain: Legowo Setiawan and Mr. Kim are responsible for the existence of the first commercial small-scale RWD drift chassis — the Atomic DRZ.

docs.title: 2018 Birth of the Atomic DRZ
docs.text:
• On January 6, 2018, Atomic officially announced the DRZ on Facebook.

  • Sales began in March.

For the first time, small-scale drifting had a dedicated RWD chassis available to the public.

docs.title: 2018 Some hobbyists started to develop their own DIY RWD and 3D-printed designs
docs.text:
January 13, 2018 — Andi Capitt presents a MINI-Z RWD kit:

Released just days after the DRZ announcement, it showcased an impressive feature set for the time — full geometry adjustability, thoughtful steering design, and clear influence from larger-scale drift concepts.

July 26, 2018 — Pebysetyana posts early prototype designs:

Later, in October 2018, he introduces the BRP:

And in December 2018, the second-generation BRP V2 appeared. It gained strong regional popularity and demonstrated how community-driven development could rival commercial platforms.

Meanwhile, more and more RWD DIY conversions and experiments for the WLToys K989 began appearing.

docs.title: May 2019 XRX Techstorm DPA
docs.text:
XRX entered the scene aggressively. Instead of focusing only on a chassis, they launched branded electronics alongside a full lineup of high-end option parts machined from 7075-T6 anodized aluminum.

This marked a shift: small scale was no longer just about experimentation — it was becoming a proper aftermarket ecosystem.

docs.title: June 2019 Super Skeeter conversion and BM Racing option parts for Atomic DRZ
docs.text:
On May 31, Atomic announced an upcoming DRZ conversion developed by Super Skeeter, with Atomic handling distribution.

Just days earlier, on June 10, BM Racing had already posted CNC-machined option parts for the DRZ platform.

On June 12, 2019, the Super Skeeter was released, offering:

  • direct-drive steering

  • wheelbase adjustment

  • quick caster adjustment

  • quick Ackermann adjustment

It quickly became one of the most recognizable DRZ-based builds of the era.

docs.title: August 2019 - BRP V3
docs.text: The third iteration of the BRP arrived — more refined, more visually striking, and widely appreciated both in Indonesia and overseas.

docs.title: September 2019 - DriftArt
docs.text:
By September 2019, the main figures of the small-scale RWD scene were already established. Then DriftArt appeared.

Developed by Mr. Chen, DriftArt stood apart immediately. Its ultra-low profile — reminiscent of an F1 car — and nylon 3D-printed construction were unlike anything else on the market at the time.

The design focused on minimalism, low center of gravity, and visual realism. MINI-Z bodies could be mounted in a way that completely concealed the chassis underneath.

From a technical standpoint, the first generation DriftArt offered an impressive set of solutions:

  • helical gears

  • a wide range of ride height adjustments

  • telescopic CVDs

  • multiple components assembled using a mortise and tenon system, allowing adjustment and disassembly without the need for tools

  • support for direct installation of a standard MINI-Z body mount

  • an integrated universal body mount, providing more adjustability

Approximately 200 units were produced and sold exclusively in China, which further contributed to its almost mythical status among collectors and early enthusiasts.

Despite its limited availability, DriftArt clearly demonstrated the direction in which small scale RWD drifting was evolving — bold design choices, innovative technologies, and an uncompromising pursuit of optimal geometry. This first generation laid the foundation for later DriftArt versions, which would go on to become some of the most influential chassis in the history of small scale RWD drifting.

What makes the DriftArt even more remarkable is that, in at least one well-known case, a long-time DriftArt collector — owning nearly every DriftArt component ever produced — openly expressed willingness to purchase a DriftArt 1 for $1,000, yet was still unable to acquire one.

As of today, it is believed that all surviving DriftArt 1 chassis remain in China, held by private collectors, further emphasizing their extreme rarity and almost inaccessible status.

docs.title: October 2019 - HGD1 and PWK
docs.text:
HGD1 wasn’t technologically groundbreaking or visually striking, but it was metal/carbon, wheelbase-adjustable, and available worldwide. RTR packages — including electronics, controller, and diecast body — were reportedly available around $250, lowering the barrier to entry for newcomers.

In parallel, PWK, designed by Pur Woko, emerged from Indonesia. Its distinctive dual-color 3D-printed design caught attention, and Indonesian driving videos continued to captivate the global community.

docs.title: November 2019 - TRC
docs.text:
TRC V1, developed by Tommy RC, represents one of the most ambitious and mysterious designs of the pre-COVID era.

Limited to just 30 units — 15 pink and 15 purple — it remains one of the rarest small-scale RWD chassis ever produced.

Its engineering was unusually complex, featuring active caster up front and a dynamically linked rear toe system tied to steering input. This level of mechanical complexity was virtually unheard of in small scale RWD designs at the time.

Due to its extremely limited production and lack of official documentation, TRC V1 remains largely undocumented and almost unseen outside of China. Its rarity, combined with its bold engineering choices, has given TRC V1 an almost mythical status within the small scale RWD drift community.

Details reported by a documented owner include an adjustable wheelbase ranging from 90 to 120 mm. The same owner has stated that the chassis was manufactured in Japan, though this has not been confirmed.

Rather than influencing the scene through widespread adoption, TRC V1 stands as a peak example of late-2019 experimental design — a chassis that pushed mechanical ideas to their limits just before small scale RWD drifting entered its next phase of rapid global expansion.

docs.title: November 2019 - Nero
docs.text: Another Indonesian 3D-printed design followed: NERO_ZUNKIES 49, created by Nockizz Ardian Troy — yet another sign of how quickly the scene was expanding.

docs.title: January 2020 - BMR-X and PWK V2
docs.text:
BM Racing transitioned from DRZ option parts to their own chassis: BMR-X.

Their strategy was different. They released the fully upgraded VIP version first. The VIP version was the most expensive small scale kit at the time, and you could get one for $429. It was not MINI-Z 1:28 scale, but 1:24 and offered with magnet mounting body posts, adjustable offset wheels, suitable for 1:24 model kit bodies. Another unique feature for the small-scale scene was its RWD layout with a front-mounted motor.

Later, they announced the BMR-X Standard edition, which would be available from June, priced at $135.

Second version of the PWK chassis was released, offered in two color trim again.

Later he implemented an important upgrade, refining the design with slimmer decks, which suits even today's standard.

docs.title: COVID-19 and the boom of home drifting
docs.text:
When the COVID-19 pandemic hit in 2020, nearly all drift tracks closed overnight.

For 1/10 scale drifting, it was a setback. For small scale, it became an unexpected accelerator.

Home layouts, DIY tracks, and online communities exploded — and small-scale RWD drifting transformed from a niche experiment into a global movement.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
Small-scale RWD drifting didn’t appear overnight.

It grew from experimentation, shared knowledge, bold design decisions, and a community willing to push boundaries — long before it became accessible or popular.

This story isn’t just about chassis. It’s about people, curiosity, and a scene that built itself from the ground up.
